sources.title: Adoption and Impact of OER in the Global South

messages.content:
### Summary: Adoption and Impact of OER in the Global South

This volume presents the findings of the **Research on Open Educational Resources for Development (ROER4D) project**, which investigated how Open Educational Resources (OER) can address educational challenges within the Global South. Published as an Open Access resource, the collection provides a critical, evidence-based perspective on the adoption and impact of openness in education across diverse contexts.

#### Key Points:

*   **Project Scope and Objectives:** Initially focused on post-secondary education in 2013, the project expanded to include basic education, government policy, and the broader integration of Open Educational Practices (OEP). It involved over 100 researchers across 21 countries.
*   **Addressing Educational Inequality:** The research focuses on the specific challenges of the Global South, including unequal access to quality resources, infrastructure disparities, and concerns regarding the sustainability and cost-effectiveness of educational systems.
*   **Conceptual Framework:** The text defines OER through the "5Rs" framework (Retain, Reuse, Revise, Remix, and Redistribute) and emphasizes the critical role of open licensing (e.g., Creative Commons) in enabling these practices.
*   **Regional Diversity:** The volume is structured into four sections covering:
    *   **Overview:** Theoretical landscapes and baseline studies.
    *   **South America:** Policy landscapes and case studies in mathematics education and teacher co-creation.
    *   **Sub-Saharan Africa:** Financing, teacher education, and MOOC integration.
    *   **South and Southeast Asia:** Cultural-historical factors, faculty attitudes, and localized impact studies.
*   **Contribution to the Field:** By providing contextually grounded research from the Global South—moving away from Northern-centric narratives—the volume offers essential insights for policymakers and practitioners. It highlights that "openness" is not a universal solution but a complex practice influenced by local culture, language barriers, and micro-politics within institutions.
*   **Commitment to Openness:** In alignment with its subject matter, the book itself is an Open Access publication, reflecting the project’s dedication to sharing knowledge and fostering academic debate beyond typical paywalled barriers.

***

**Reference Sources (Document Sections):**
*   Project Overview: [S.6], [S.18]-[S.21]
*   Context and Rationale: [S.11]-[S.13]
*   Editorial and Institutional Background: [S.4], [S.8]-[S.10]

Lesson 5 — Adoption and Impact of OER in the Global South - Presentation5 slides
Adoption and Impact of OER in the Global South
  • A comprehensive academic study on Open Educational Resources (OER).
  • Focuses on research, implementation, and impact within the Global South.

The provided document serves as the front matter for the book titled *Adoption and Impact of OER in the Global South*, edited by Cheryl Hodgkinson-Williams and Patricia B. Arinto.

The following points summarize the key information:

Editorial and Authorship
The volume is edited by Cheryl Hodgkinson-Williams and Patricia B. Arinto [S.1, S.4]. It is a product of the Research on Open Educational Resources for Development (ROER4D) project [S.4, S.5].

Peer Review
Chapters 2 through 15 underwent review by at least two external reviewers, while the introduction (Chapter 1) and conclusion (Chapter 16) were not peer-reviewed [S.5].

Publication and Licensing
The book was published in 2017 through a collaboration between African Minds and the International Development Research Centre (IDRC) [S.4, S.5]. It is primarily licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International licence, with the exception of Chapter 12, which follows a Creative Commons Attribution-ShareAlike International licence [S.5].

Support and Funding
The research presented was supported by a grant from the IDRC, although the views expressed in the text do not necessarily reflect the position of the IDRC or its Board of Governors [S.5].

Citation Guidance
For formal referencing, the editors recommend the following format: Hodgkinson-Williams, C. & Arinto, P. B. (2017). *Adoption and impact of OER in the Global South*. Cape Town & Ottawa: African Minds, International Development Research Centre & Research on Open Educational Resources. The text is officially registered with the DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.1005330 [S.5].

This document outlines the contents and acknowledgements of *Adoption and Impact of OER in the Global South*, a volume documenting the research projects of the ROER4D network.

### Structure of the Volume [S.6-7]
The book is organized into five distinct sections:
*   **Section 1: Overview:** Introduces the project landscape, meta-synthesis of factors influencing OER, and baseline survey data regarding instructors.
*   **Section 2: South America:** Examines policy landscapes in Chile, Colombia, and Uruguay, the co-creation of materials, and the effectiveness of OER in mathematical higher education.
*   **Section 3: Sub-Saharan Africa:** Focuses on educational funding in South Africa, pedagogic change in East Africa, factors shaping lecturer adoption, and the role of OER in MOOCs.
*   **Section 4: South and Southeast Asia:** Explores cultural-historical factors in Mongolia, faculty perceptions in India, integrating OER into teacher education in Sri Lanka, collaborative adoption approaches in Karnataka, and localized impact studies in Afghanistan.
*   **Section 5: Conclusion:** Provides recommendations regarding social inclusion in the context of Open Educational Resources (OER) and Open Educational Practices (OEP).

### Acknowledgements [S.8-9]
The ROER4D Network Hub acknowledges the following key contributors to the volume:
*   **Founders and Partners:** Institutional support came from the University of Cape Town and Wawasan Open University, with primary funding provided by Canada’s IDRC, the UK’s Department for International Development, and the Open Society Foundations.
*   **Governance and Development:** Recognition is given to the original planning group, project curators, and editorial team who guided the research from inception through to the final publication.
*   **Reviewers and Contributors:** A diverse international panel of peer reviewers and organizational partners provided the intellectual scaffolding and contractual support necessary for the project’s success.

This document provides an overview of the Research on Open Educational Resources for Development (ROER4D) project, its leadership, and its mission to critically evaluate OER in the Global South.

**Editors and Project Leadership**
The project was led by Associate Professor Cheryl Hodgkinson-Williams from the University of Cape Town and Professor Patricia Brazil Arinto from the University of the Philippines – Open University. Their combined expertise in ICT in education and technology-supported learning provided the foundation for the ROER4D network ([S.10]).

**Core Objectives and Critical Reflection**
Tel Amiel (UNESCO Chair in Open Education) highlights the project's importance in moving beyond the "hype" of OER. Key points of inquiry include:
- Questioning the definition, enactment, and impact of "openness" in education ([S.11]).
- Addressing gaps such as language barriers, the lack of culturally adapted resources, and the persistent digital divide ([S.11], [S.12]).
- Examining the ethics of production and the micro-politics of institutional demands regarding OER ([S.12]).
- Challenging the assumption that mainstream OER trajectories inherently lead to emancipatory or democratic educational outcomes ([S.11]).

**The ROER4D Network and Impact**
Supported by the IDRC and UKaid, the network involved over 100 researchers across 21 countries. Matthew Smith of the IDRC notes that the project's success went beyond traditional research to include:
- Influencing tangible educational policy, such as supporting OER policy in Sri Lanka and state-sponsored portals in India ([S.13]).
- Experimenting with open, collaborative, and inclusive research practices throughout the project lifecycle ([S.13]).
- Creating an open-access publication that provides a rich, evidence-based tapestry of insights for those interested in the global advancement of Open Education ([S.13], [S.14]).

### Summary: Research on Open Educational Resources (OER) in the Global South

The provided document outlines the scope and context of the ROER4D (Research on Open Educational Resources for Development) project, which investigates the adoption and impact of OER within the Global South.

#### Project Background and Objectives
The ROER4D project, launched in 2013 with support from IDRC, DFID, and OSF, examines how OER can address the demand for accessible, high-quality, and affordable education. While initially focused on post-secondary education, the research scope expanded to include basic education and the potential impact of Open Educational Practices (OEP) [S.19].

#### Defining OER and Core Principles
OER refers to teaching and learning materials in the public domain or under intellectual property licenses that permit free use and repurposing. The project utilizes Wiley’s "5Rs" framework to define the utility of open content [S.20]:
*   Retain: Make and own copies.
*   Reuse: Use content in various ways.
*   Revise: Adapt or modify the content.
*   Remix: Combine original or revised content.
*   Redistribute: Share copies with others.

#### Educational Challenges in the Global South
Research is driven by three interconnected challenges that hinder educational equity in the Global South [S.21]:

1.  Unequal Access: Despite significant growth in student enrollment, institutional funding has stagnated. Infrastructure deficits—such as poor internet connectivity, lack of computer labs, and textbook shortages—are often exacerbated by geographic (urban versus rural), gender, and socioeconomic disparities [S.21, S.22].
2.  Variable Quality: Increased access has not always translated into improved student performance. Common issues include poorly qualified teaching staff, a lack of professional development, and reliance on outdated or contextually inappropriate instructional materials often imported from the Global North [S.23, S.24].
3.  Sustainability and Costs: The rise of privatization and increased higher education demands place a heavy financial burden on students. These rising costs and the need for rigorous quality assurance mechanisms in rapidly expanding systems highlight the importance of evaluating OER as a sustainable educational intervention [S.23, S.24].

#### The Role of Research
Because most existing OER research originates from the Global North, the ROER4D project aims to bridge the knowledge gap by investigating the impact of OER within the specific sociocultural, linguistic, and technical contexts of the Global South [S.20, S.21].

This section, spanning pages 69 to 186, focuses on the baseline usage of Open Educational Resources (OER) in the Global South, the regional policy landscape in Latin America, and collaborative practices in education.

**OER Usage in Higher Education**
Chapter 3 presents a baseline survey of higher education instructors to assess the current state of OER adoption. This study provides a foundational understanding of how educators are utilizing open resources and identifies the primary constraints they face, setting the stage for more granular regional analysis.

**Regional Policy and Practice in Latin America**
Chapters 4 through 5 examine the adoption of OER within South America, specifically focusing on:

* Policy Landscapes: Researchers investigated the regulatory environments in Chile, Colombia, and Uruguay. The findings highlight how national policies regarding Open Access and OER influence institutional behavior and instructor autonomy.
* Collaborative Co-creation: In Colombia, the focus shifts to the active engagement of teachers and teacher educators. The research emphasizes the collaborative development of OER, noting that the act of co-creation serves as a significant driver for both pedagogical improvement and the sustainable adoption of open practices.

**Core Findings**
The research identifies a strong connection between institutional support and the willingness of faculty to engage with open materials. Furthermore, the text underscores that successful integration depends not only on the availability of resources but on the cultural and pedagogical readiness of the educators within these specific geographic contexts. The collaborative approach seen in teacher education programs is identified as a particularly effective model for overcoming traditional barriers to OER adoption.

This section of the volume focuses on the empirical research and case studies concerning the adoption of Open Educational Resources (OER) within the Sub-Saharan African context.

Key points include:

Financial Transparency: Chapter 7 investigates the challenges of tracking funding directed toward OER in South African basic education, highlighting a critical knowledge gap in budget transparency and resource allocation.

Pedagogical Change: Chapter 8 explores the role of teacher educators in East Africa, emphasizing how OER adoption acts as a catalyst for shifting pedagogical practices within the classroom.

Institutional Factors: Chapter 9 identifies the specific organizational and individual factors that influence lecturers' decisions to adopt OER at three distinct South African universities. The research suggests that adoption is rarely a straightforward process and is heavily contingent upon institutional culture and support.

Integrated Models: Chapter 10 examines the intersection of OER and Massive Open Online Courses (MOOCs), analyzing how these formats can leverage open content to expand educational reach.

Overall, the section underscores the complex interplay between institutional policy, lecturer attitudes, and the practical challenges of implementation in diverse educational environments across the continent.

This section (Chapters 10–15) focuses on the empirical evidence regarding the adoption and impact of Open Educational Resources (OER) across Sub-Saharan Africa and Asia. The key points are structured as follows:

**Regional Integration and Case Studies**
*   **Sub-Saharan Africa:** Chapter 10 explores the intersection of OER and Massive Open Online Courses (MOOCs), examining how these formats can facilitate content delivery and reach.
*   **Central and South Asia:** Chapters 11, 12, 14, and 15 detail the diverse factors impacting OER. In Mongolia, adoption is heavily influenced by cultural-historical contexts (p. 389). In India, research highlights how faculty attitudes, perceptions of quality, and structural barriers determine engagement, while the use of teacher professional learning communities fosters collaborative adoption (pp. 425, 499). In Afghanistan, study findings reflect on the early-stage impact of localizing educational materials (p. 549).
*   **Southeast Asia:** Chapter 13 provides an assessment of how integrating OER into teacher education frameworks at the Open University of Sri Lanka affects educational outcomes and institutional practices (p. 459).

**Common Themes and Drivers**
*   **Pedagogic Change:** A recurring theme is that OER adoption is not merely about access to content but about driving shifts in teaching methodology and teacher professional development.
*   **Barriers to Adoption:** Across these regions, adoption is shaped by a complex interplay of motivation, perceived quality of the resources, institutional support, and localized cultural factors.
*   **Collaborative Models:** The evidence suggests that professional learning communities are effective vehicles for scaling OER adoption, as they move beyond individual lecturer use toward collective educational improvement.

The following summary covers the final section of the volume, specifically focusing on Chapter 16, which addresses the conclusions and recommendations for the Open Educational Resources (OER) and Open Educational Practices (OEP) landscape in the Global South (p. 577).

**Core Findings on OER Adoption**
Research indicates that while OER adoption in the Global South is technically feasible, success is heavily mediated by socio-economic and cultural factors. The studies demonstrate that OER are not a universal panacea; their effectiveness depends on institutional infrastructure, the professional development of educators, and the perceived quality of materials within local contexts.

**Key Challenges to Social Inclusion**
The text highlights that the digital divide remains a primary barrier to equitable OER access. Furthermore, there is a recurring tension between globalized educational content and the need for localized (or vernacular) resources. Without intentional efforts to bridge these gaps, OER initiatives risk reinforcing existing inequalities rather than facilitating social inclusion.

**Recommendations for Policy and Practice**
The authors propose several strategic shifts to improve the impact of OER:
1. Institutional Policy: Organizations must move beyond ad-hoc OER adoption toward integrated strategies that incentivize faculty participation and reward open pedagogical practices.
2. Capacity Building: Professional learning communities are essential for sustaining change. Policies should prioritize supporting educators in co-creating and adapting resources rather than merely consuming them.
3. Funding Models: Sustainability requires tracking the financial impact and ensuring that funding is directed toward localized content creation and student-centered support systems.
4. Future Research: Further studies are needed to evaluate the long-term impact on student learning outcomes and the broader role of OER in enhancing global knowledge justice.

In summary, the concluding chapter emphasizes that achieving the promise of open education in the Global South requires shifting the focus from the resources themselves to the pedagogical practices and institutional policies that empower diverse learners and educators.
Here is a quiz for this lesson.

questions.question: According to Tel Amiel’s foreword, what is a primary limitation of viewing the OER movement through a "Whig-like" narrative?
options: It assumes that widespread adoption will naturally lead to universal, emancipatory, and democratic outcomes.
It focuses too heavily on the role of surveillance economy businesses like Google.
It suggests that open licensing is not an essential element of the OER movement.
It ignores the importance of high-quality scholarly research in the Global South.
questions.explanation: Amiel warns against the assumption of inevitable progress ('Whig-like' narrative), noting that without critical review, we risk ignoring fundamental questions about who truly benefits and if OER is inherently inclusive.

questions.question: What is identified as a significant gap in the current OER landscape discussed in the ROER4D research?
options: A lack of interest from institutional policymakers.
The absence of digital learning management systems in most regions.
The availability of resources in diverse or local languages, or those adapted to specific cultural contexts.
The refusal of faculty to engage with intellectual property rights.
questions.explanation: The text highlights that the lack of resources in multiple (especially non-hegemonic) languages and the insufficient adaptation to local cultural contexts are key challenges identified by the studies.

questions.question: How did the ROER4D project specifically aim to shift the perspective of OER research?
options: By creating research strictly for the benefit of international NGOs.
By focusing exclusively on the technological hurdles of MOOCs.
By ensuring research was conducted by Southern researchers specifically for their local contexts.
By standardizing OER policies to follow North American models.
questions.explanation: Matthew Smith’s foreword emphasizes that the vision was to improve educational opportunities by supporting high-quality research conducted by researchers from the Global South, for their own contexts.

questions.question: Based on the foreword by Tel Amiel, why might an educator have 'good reasons' to avoid using a very liberal license for their work?
options: They are technically incapable of understanding Creative Commons licenses.
They have concerns regarding the ethics of production and the interests of the groups (such as surveillance companies) using their work.
Liberal licenses are considered illegal in most Southern hemisphere countries.
Liberal licenses prevent the educator from receiving any academic credit for their teaching.
questions.explanation: Amiel cautions that a 'naive' view of openness ignores conflicting interests; he mentions that educators may have legitimate ethical, cultural, or political reasons for hesitating to use liberal licenses.

questions.question: Which of the following best characterizes the scope of the ROER4D project’s research as presented in the content?
options: A centralized set of findings focused only on the effectiveness of MOOCs.
A purely theoretical academic review without practical application.
A multi-regional exploration covering cultural, policy, and institutional factors in OER adoption.
A collection of case studies limited solely to higher education in South Africa.
questions.explanation: The table of contents and forewords describe a wide-ranging project that covers various geographic regions (South America, Sub-Saharan Africa, Asia) and explores diverse factors like culture, policy, and institutional demands.

cards.front: What volume provides a comprehensive study on the adoption and impact of OER in the Global South?
cards.back: Adoption and Impact of OER in the Global South, edited by Cheryl Hodgkinson-Williams and Patricia B. Arinto.

cards.front: What is the acronym for the project that conducted this research on OER?
cards.back: ROER4D (Research on Open Educational Resources for Development).

cards.front: Who are the primary editors of the volume 'Adoption and Impact of OER in the Global South'?
cards.back: Cheryl Hodgkinson-Williams and Patricia B. Arinto.

cards.front: What is the main mission of the African Minds peer-reviewed academic books list?
cards.back: To support the dissemination of African scholarship and foster access, openness, and debate.

cards.front: According to Tel Amiel, what is one of the most significant contributions of this volume?
cards.back: It presents detailed descriptions of OER initiatives in regions often presented only in the aggregate or in negative terms.

cards.front: What was the initial vision of the ROER4D network?
cards.back: To improve educational opportunities by supporting high-quality, impactful research by researchers from the South for the diverse contexts where the research was conducted.

cards.front: Which international organization provided the grant for the research presented in this volume?
cards.back: The International Development Research Centre (IDRC), Ottawa, Canada.

cards.front: What does Matthew Smith highlight as a strategic success of the ROER4D network beyond just research?
cards.back: The network influenced OER policies (e.g., in Sri Lanka) and implemented state-sponsored OER portals for teacher professional development (e.g., in India).

cards.front: Which licensing regime, generally speaking, applies to the chapters in this volume?
cards.back: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International licence (CC BY 4.0).

cards.front: According to the foreword, what is a recurring challenge regarding OER in the Global South that is often brushed aside?
cards.back: The lack of resources in local and non-hegemonic languages and the need for appropriately adapted resources.

slides.title: Adoption and Impact of OER in the Global South
bullets: A comprehensive academic study on Open Educational Resources (OER).
Focuses on research, implementation, and impact within the Global South.
slides.notes: This volume is part of the ROER4D project, supporting African scholarship and open access. It explores how OER influences education in developing regions.

slides.title: Structure of the Volume
bullets: Covers diverse regions: South America, Africa, and Asia (p. 6-7).
Features forewords by global experts like Tel Amiel (p. 6).
Peer-reviewed academic contributions on open education (p. 5).
slides.notes: The book is organized into five sections, ranging from project landscapes to regional case studies and final policy recommendations.

slides.title: Critical Perspectives on Openness
bullets: Moves beyond the hype cycle of open education (p. 11).
Questions if openness leads to truly equitable education (p. 11).
Examines the digital divide and local context limitations (p. 11-12).
Highlights the need for culturally adapted, multilingual resources (p. 11).
slides.notes: Tel Amiel emphasizes that we must look beyond license types to ensure OER addresses real-world, localized educational needs.

slides.title: The ROER4D Research Project
bullets: Investigates OER adoption to improve education quality and access (p. 19).
Expanded scope: post-secondary, basic education, and policy impact (p. 19).
Funded by IDRC, DFID, and Open Society Foundations (p. 19).
Builds on previous work like the PANdora project (p. 19).
slides.notes: The project aim was to identify how and under what conditions OER can effectively meet the demand for relevant education in the Global South.

slides.title: Defining OER and its Frameworks
bullets: OER are public domain or openly licensed teaching resources (p. 19-20).
Terminology varies across Global South languages (p. 20).
Wiley’s '5Rs': Retain, Reuse, Revise, Remix, and Redistribute (p. 20).
Open licensing, like Creative Commons, is essential to OER (p. 20).
slides.notes: Understanding OER requires both a clear legal definition provided by open licenses and a functional definition of how content can be modified and shared.
